sentences.text: The life in the ghetto was concentrated primarily-- at that time in 1940, the ghetto was established in late October after the-- high-- high Jewish holidays.
From some parts of Warsaw, you had to move.
People who were living within this-- this district, they had the-- the-- the-- the comfort of not being-- no, not having the need to move their belongings or anything.
And they were right in the right place.
Just fortunately that we were living in the ghetto district.
There was a quite convenient place, because many of these streetworkers crossing like Gasia Street, spelled G-A-S-I-A, today on 11th Street, and Dr.
Zamenhof Street that's spelled Z-A-M-E-N-H-O-F Street, the founder of Esperanto.
There were many other streets, quite famous.
Nalewki, ?] the center of the bustling [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] industry of-- of-- similar to the Lower East Side in New York.
And so these things were providing sufficient amount of dealing with one another.
They didn't need outside contact yet.
There-- there were quite a bit of outside contact through these Jewish people and the Polish people, and they were comin' and going.
But mostly that was-- dealings were done-- were done between one another.
With my mother and we-- in a rented place.
In the--
After they established the ghetto.
We were right in the center of it.
They were renting another two rooms to some people who came from the other part of Warsaw where they had to leave their quarters.
But then they found some other quarters and they wanted to be independent.
It was not easy to find, but for people who had some kind of financial comfort, they could find whatever they needed.
There were many people that were giving up the sake of getting something, some monetary help, and they were moving with the families under severe condition, and it was getting to be very, very tight.
There were-- one together was a [? salvage ?] people, had to move very fast.
They had limited time.
Similar to this what we had in Lodz, 24 hours and you would be [? ghetto. ?] As a matter of fact, there's some of the walls around the ghetto were built overnight, and that was quite an achievement.
We were not used to see any such thing done so rapidly.
We also realize that the Germans were totally devoted to destruction and control of the people in the ghetto.
There were a number of gates established in the ghetto, and from the gates they-- people were coming or going to work, or the passages for Germans.
And one of the passages was the quite famous Karmelizka Street, spelled C-A-- K-A-R-M-E-L-I-Z-K-A, which was connecting [INAUDIBLE] with [?
Dzielna. ?] And this was a thoroughfare where the tram was passing by.
And later, when the Germans did not allow any more electric trams, was horse driven tram.
And then they eliminated even this because it was a route of the Gestapo wagons to-- to the [?
Dzielna ?] Street [?
Pawiak. ?] And people were concerned about it because when they were passing by, there were many casualties.
They were running people over on the side without any--
The Gestapo people transporting the prisoners to [? jail, ?] to [?
Pawiak ?] [?
Street. ?] And--
Oh, quite often.
As a matter of fact, not far away from the [?
Dzielna ?] Street, from the Karmelizka Street, who-- I met [INAUDIBLE] in 1940, and he encouraged me to join the Hashomer Hatzair organization in Warsaw.
And they-- how I contacted-- through him, I contacted the outside war, outside of ghetto, organization of PPS.
PPS is the Polish Party of-- Socialist Party.
I volunteered to conduct for them services of distributing illegal newspapers to some of the people, known only to the PPS, and-- who wanted to receive news directly printed by the organization.
And they listened to the BBC, British Broadcasting Corporation.
And their news were printed night, at night, and delivered during the day or the following day.
According to the habits of the people living outside of the ghetto, they knew that they had to be very careful because they were always followed by somebody.
And we learned the tricks of finding out if anybody is followed or watched by the agent of Gestapo.
I did realize that Germany status has to be undermined.
And people have to learn how-- what news are coming from the outside world to spread among the population by mouth.
So chosen people were getting newsletters from the party for the benefit of the other people who were gathering or meeting together.
Meetings, when I mean meetings, is contact of good friends who occasionally met in coffeehouse or met on the street, walk for some time.
And then they were not allowed any meetings, official meetings of any parties or anything unless they were pro-Nazis and they had the approval of the German authority.
PPS?
PPS?
No, they were strictly Polish party.

And I had a hardship at the beginning, but then, with my knowledge of Polish and my way of expressing myself in Polish, they accepted me and warned me and guided me how to respond in case I would be caught by Gestapo and what can happen.
Well, there was mistrust because they knew that I was Jewish.
And they were afraid that I might want to be-- want to penetrate their organization for the purpose of serving the German.
But then, oh yes, every-- every step was very carefully treated.
They knew that people disappeared overnight for any atrocities against German authorities.
Originally I knew some people that I knew from before the war, through certain people that were reading in the newspapers.
And then when I--
Kwiatkowski.
Spelled K-W-I-A-T-K-O-W-S-K-I.
I didn't know him.
I just know-- knew about him, that he was active in Polish Socialist Party.
And when I met [INAUDIBLE], I asked about him, and he said he will see what he can do.
And I told him that I-- what was the purpose of my-- why I was asking and wanting to get to him.
And a few days later when I met him again, [INAUDIBLE] said, I think that I can arrange it for you.
And he told me precisely where to go and what time to the Polish side of the Warsaw that was not yet a ghetto.
This was sometime in August or beginning of September.
And without any identification, I went to these people.
When I met the man, I spoke to this individual from behind a curtain.
I didn't see his face, I never met him.
And of course, I went through questioning.
And then I find that he was not alone, but there were another three men.
And finally they told me where to go, what to do, and how to pick up the material, how to distribute.
And this happened after they told me, we'll give you one dry run, testing me if I can really cope with the problem.
And as evidently I was very devoted, and I was trying very hard to pass the test.
And from that moment on, I knew that there is somebody who is always assisting me.
But I didn't know that it was a girl, or a boy, or a man, or whoever to overlook over my activities in distributing the leaflets.
The leaflets were wrapped around milk bottles and left at certain points for people to pick up.
I was distributing these bottles on a-- on a bicycle, with a little [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] basket in front, from place to place.
And these bottles were wrapped in the news from the BBC.
There was in a few areas, they would constantly change.
There was one area around the very, very treacherous Marszalkowska Street.
You probably remember this street from-- Marszalkowska was the-- the street of marshals.
And it was named after the Marshal Pilsudski.
And then there was one section, the other section was [INAUDIBLE], [INAUDIBLE] was very close to the Vistula River.
And if you knew the area, then you just know where to drop off the the bottles at what time.
I didn't know the people, I just knew where to drop it off.
The addresses.
They-- they were-- they were telling you from day to day.
When you were picking up the bottles, then you were-- then you were told.
You had to learn how to remember addresses.
Without it, you could not exist.
You-- they would not give you any assignment.
You had to-- if you hesitated, if you would ask, oh, is this [? at ?] [? so-- ?] that was not good.
They would take the bottles away from you.
You had to respond immediately without any hesitation.
15 bottles a day.
And this was when I was very, very satisfied after being able to deliver for a number of days these bottles, until one day there was a raid and, accidentally, I got involved in it.
Then got caught by.
Oh, for about four to six months, probably six months.
That was even--
During the wintertime.
Even the ghetto was established, I had the possibility of getting with the work in columns from the ghetto, and disappear and do my work, come back with the working column back together.
There were payoffs.
And the-- the foremen of such column knew who will join where and when.
Working column, return to the ghetto with the working column.
I had to be, be always ready to return to the ghetto at the proper time, otherwise I couldn't join in on the column.
It was not a simple assignment, but it was very gratifying, very happy.
I can't imagine my mother's heart when I was returning home from-- from such mission.
Yes, she did, and she approved of it.
And we started to hear all the atrocities of Germans doing to the Jewish people, and then we decided that this is perhaps the one way of fighting back.
And it somehow worked out.
In the meantime, I also [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] joined the Hashomer Hatzair [INAUDIBLE] and this is the first time that I met [?
Anielewicz. ?] And--
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I know it was 1940, before the ghetto was established, where I was doing all this work.
I had never told him that I was doing the work for the PPS.
And [INAUDIBLE], when he introduced me to [?
Anielewicz, ?] he mentioned to him, do not ask him too many questions.
He is one of us and take my word for it.
And that's how I remain.
But when later around when I-- after I was caught in [INAUDIBLE] [?
Pawiak, ?] and prior to the outbreak of the uprising in ghetto, [?
Anielewicz ?] found out what I was [? on. ?]
He had-- he organized a small group of Hashomer Hatzair.
Hashomer Hatzair was a scouting organization.
And his prime interests were to build up a youth group which would be willing to go to work on farm systems in Palestine at that time.
Without having any hesitation and need of looking for something extra special, but to build a land for the future of the people.
And the group was very, very small, 14 people.
They were meeting on a weekly basis, primarily on Saturday or Sunday, and--
We were meeting in attics or very remote place, neglected place, in the ghetto, on Leszno Street, spelled L-E-S-Z-N-O.
Yeah, [?
Handelsman. ?] There were two people from [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] college, from City College.
They also left the [INAUDIBLE] and came to Warsaw.
His name was [?
Baharier. ?] I forgot the first name.
There was [INAUDIBLE].
There was [INAUDIBLE].
There was [?
Benny Rosen ?].
A number of these people.
There was [?
Vukovich, ?] there was-- I did remember many more, but I guess with age I'm forgetting.
People were very young age.
Maybe, perhaps of the same age as I was, of 17 and-- and 19.
Anielewicz, ?] perhaps, was 21 or 22 at that time.
I beg your pardon?
A very, very pleasant individual that, when you-- when you confronted him, you felt that you have a friend immediately, a friend that you can trust.
And he did not ask you too many questions, especially when you were recommended by a man like [INAUDIBLE].
And of course, when he approached me he asked me in Hebrew if I understand Hebrew.
And I said to him, he can freely talk to me in Hebrew.
And he was very happy about it, and we established our friendship right-- the night of the very first meeting.
And-- however, if there were possibilities of going on some of the Polish farms for training, which were still allowed by the Germans, as working people on the farms, I could not participate.
And the-- the reason for it was I was already engaged in-- in working in [INAUDIBLE].
[INAUDIBLE], this was the time when I was caught by the Gestapo and brought to the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] prison of Pawiak for interrogation.
All right.
Yes, there were.
I don't remember her.
There were two girls there.
There was one, [?
Gutah, ?] and another one was [?
Helena. ?] And-- but there were two more.
But there was a very well knit group of people who were talking seriously about settling in-- in Palestine and conducting their duty as a future citizen of Israel.
I was--
About from August, for about six months.
Every couple days.
There was never the same, constantly the same day.
Because not always they could get the news from BBC and print it, so there was some chance, sometimes three days, sometimes four days.
Every-- every fourth day or every third day.
And the funny part, I would like to emphasize that I never met the man who interrogated me at the very first time.
And he said to me that if I would be ever approached, to respond only to the name Tadk, which means T-A-D-K.
And then, that's how I was.

It is a matter of fact that the girl that I knew was supervising this district or whatever, she once approached me asking me if I need any food and things like this, because there was a really harsh day of distributing all the bottles because of the interferences of German movements of soldiers and some other groups.
Jadzia.
I don't know the-- spelled J-A-D-Z-I-A.
No.
I didn't even have contact with her.
I just knew that wherever I'm going, under different dressing, she will be supervising it.
Some time, when I spotted for some movements, she would even dress as a-- a-- a nurse, and the next time she was dressed as a elderly lady.
And just conducting her work.
And I felt quite comfortable when I knew that there was somebody around, of overlooking what was going on.
It was a sort of protection of my own self.
Then one day, during the raid-- accidentally raid-- I was caught in a raid and--
Well, the Germans closed some streets and tightened up the entrances to the streets, and people who were male, people had to go out to the streets and they put them on a wagon and brought them to [?
Aleja ?] Szucha.
They didn't ask many questions, they didn't hit anybody or anything.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [?

Aleja ?] Szucha, spelled S-Z-U-C-H-A, was a former ministry of education and the cultural affairs and religious affairs of the Polish government that was converted by the Gestapo headquarters-- to the Gestapo headquarter in Warsaw.
For the Warsaw--
They didn't ask questions.
They-- they just took me, they brought me in and during the interrogation they asked for names that I never heard, that I couldn't deliver them.
Because the beating was outstanding.
If I have a scar on my face from the-- from the accident, I had worse scars before being beaten up.
And I had my share of beating by the interrogators in the war-- in the Gestapo.
Yes.
I remember quite well the names.
There was a main interrogator, [GERMAN] Staff Sergeant Schilling.
A huge man, obese man.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] whenever he hit someone-- and he hit me a good number of times-- I-- I felt it quite well.
I was-- must have been bouncing like a ball when he was hitting me.
And, of course, he would lose-- as many times as they hit you, that you'd lose your consciousness.
And I woke up in that cellar in the compound, and they had then Gestapo.
And whatever saved me was the dampness of this compound, that it was cold and watery.
So I held my hands, of course, against the floor, and then put it on my face and I came to myself.
And somehow I was surviving and transported back to Pawiak and to this [? later ?] again brought the [INAUDIBLE] Gestapo.
That was going on like this for two months until--
They was constantly asking about names that I never heard.
In relation to the-- to the underground movement.
They did not ask.
They just knew that I'm a Jew, whatever I was doing on the Polish side.
And--
Yeah, they could find out right away by pulling the pants down.
But this didn't stop them from asking questions about the underground activities.
And after this period of time, I was transported from [INAUDIBLE] to Pawiak and back from Pawiak to [INAUDIBLE] to Karmelizka Street, to where I mentioned before.
And in Pawiak, there were a number of people already there.
And when I got--
Beg your pardon?
People that I met, but I never knew them.
There was a difference between a visual contact and actual contact with the people approaching one another.
And of course, you were assigned to a-- to a [? camper ?] with other 10 people, and it was an uncomfortable way of sleeping or living or keeping clean.
You-- only thing that help you was the daily marching outside for a half an hour in the morning and half an hour before, in the afternoon.
Otherwise, people were beaten up and how-- somehow was dragging my feet, after all these beatings.
And the only thing I remember there is [INAUDIBLE] to drink the water, I was putting it out on my face and my cut.
It was not a simple thing, encounter, for simple reason you still did hope that you somehow will live through and be able to think about it in a different manner.
